title: Product BOM Decomposition: GfS Türwächter IoT

sections.title: Market: Data Processing / Data Centers (NAICS 518210)
sections.content:
*Steps B.1–B.4 per Clayton BOM Decomposition spec. Input: 01_product_decomposition.md, 02_functional_promise.md, 03_constraints.md, 05_market_discovery.md.*

---

sections.title: NAICS Routing
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Target market NAICS** | 518210 — Computing Infrastructure Providers, Data Processing, Web Hosting, and Related Services (service sector) |
| **Product NAICS** | 332999 — Miscellaneous Fabricated Metal + Electronic Assembly (security hardware) |
| **BOM applies?** | YES — BOM is of the **Türwächter device itself** (manufactured physical product with compositional structure), deployed into this market |
| **Output type** | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring |
| **Market-specific relevance** | Data centers have Faraday-cage-like RF environments (raised floors, metal cable trays, shielded server enclosures) that attenuate sub-GHz signals. IP-native integration with DCIM/BMS is mandatory for SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / PCI-DSS audit-log requirements. Physical security zoning (perimeter → anteroom/mantrap → server room) means exits per zone must each be independently monitored and tamper-evident. |

> **Process VN vs BOM boundary:** VN covers the data center's physical security infrastructure (access control systems, mantrap operations, DCIM platforms). BOM covers what is physically inside the Türwächter device a customer purchases. The device ends up IN the door installation — it is not the process that monitors the data center.

---

sections.title: B.1 — L4 Subsystems
sections.content:
Output offering (L5): **Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT** — the sellable unit. BOM starts one level below.

| L4 Subsystem | Pattern category | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical security subsystem | Primary functional | Physically prevents handle depression; single-motion swing-release per EN 179/1125; key override — entire function is mechanical, zero power dependency |
| Alarm generation subsystem | Auxiliary | Produces 95 dB audible alarm on guard activation — mechanically triggered, zero power dependency (C2 absolute constraint satisfied) |
| IoT connectivity subsystem | Control / interface | 868 MHz wireless state broadcasting; guard-open, alarm-triggered, battery-low events; in data centers this subsystem faces the highest demand for IP-native API output |
| Power subsystem | Auxiliary | Supplies 9 V DC to IoT module and pre-alarm electronics; mechanical alarm needs none |
| Enclosure / mounting subsystem | Containment | Houses both physical subsystems; mounts to existing door handle without structural modification; data centers may require tamper-evident variant |

**Augmentation check:** All four pattern categories present. No packaging subsystem (device IS the package on the door). Containment = enclosure/mounting. No separate structural frame (self-contained device).

---

sections.title: B.2 — Full Decomposition (L4 → L0)
sections.content:
```
EXIT CONTROL 179/1125 WITH IoT  (L5 — sellable product)
│
├── L4  Mechanical security subsystem
│   ├── L3  Handle guard mechanism
│   │   ├── L2  Guard arm assembly
│   │   │   ├── L1  Guard arm (formed/stamped)
│   │   │   ├── L1  Return spring (coil)
│   │   │   └── L1  Pivot pin
│   │   └── L2  Pre-alarm contact assembly
│   │       ├── L1  Micro-switch (touch-trigger)
│   │       └── L0  Phosphor bronze (contact spring material)
│   └── L3  Key override assembly
│       ├── L1  Profile half-cylinder (lock core)
│       ├── L1  Locking cam
│       └── L0  Brass (cylinder body)
│           ← data centers often upgrade to high-security
│             dimple cylinder at this L3 position
│
├── L4  Alarm generation subsystem
│   ├── L3  Piezoelectric horn module
│   │   ├── L2  Transducer assembly
│   │   │   ├── L1  Piezo disc (ceramic wafer)
│   │   │   ├── L1  Metal resonator plate (brass/Al)
│   │   │   └── L0  PZT ceramic (lead zirconate titanate)
│   │   └── L2  Acoustic horn housing
│   │       └── L0  ABS polymer
│   └── L3  Drive circuit board
│       ├── L1  PCB (simple oscillator circuit)
│       └── L0  FR4 epoxy laminate
│
├── L4  IoT connectivity subsystem
│   ├── L3  RF transmitter module (868 MHz EasyWave)
│   │   ├── L2  RF PCB assembly
│   │   │   ├── L1  Sub-GHz RF transceiver IC
│   │   │   ├── L1  Microcontroller (MCU)
│   │   │   ├── L1  Antenna element (PCB trace or whip)
│   │   │   └── L0  FR4 epoxy laminate / copper
│   │   └── L2  State-detection assembly
│   │       └── L1  Reed switch / hall-effect sensor
│   └── L3  (Required) BUS gateway / protocol bridge
│           ← in data centers this component is mandatory,
│             not optional; bridges EasyWave → BACnet/IP or REST
│             [same L2/L1/L0 as RF PCB assembly; sells separately]
│
├── L4  Power subsystem
│   └── L3  Battery module
│       ├── L2  Battery holder assembly
│       │   ├── L1  Battery clip / cradle (injection-molded)
│       │   └── L1  Contact springs
│       └── L0  9 V battery cell (consumable — alkaline or lithium)
│           ← data center preference: lithium for longer life
│             and stable voltage in temperature-controlled rooms
│
└── L4  Enclosure / mounting subsystem
    ├── L3  Main housing module
    │   ├── L2  Housing shell assembly
    │   │   ├── L1  Front shell
    │   │   ├── L1  Back plate
    │   │   └── L0  ABS or PC/ABS blend
    │   │       ← data centers may prefer zinc die-cast for
    │   │         tamper-evidence (C5 surface-mount accepted here)
    │   └── L2  Pivot / detent assembly (45° swing hinge)
    │       ├── L1  Hinge pin
    │       ├── L1  Detent ball + spring
    │       └── L0  Steel
    └── L3  Door handle mount
        ├── L2  Mounting bracket assembly
        │   ├── L1  Bracket plate
        │   ├── L1  Fasteners (tamper-resistant hex, security torx)
        │   └── L0  Steel or aluminum
        └── L2  Glass-frame plate (Art. 991170 — optional)
            └── L0  Steel
```

---

sections.title: B.3 — Alternatives at Every Level
sections.content:
### Position 1 — L4 Alarm Generation: Acoustic Transducer Technology

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric disc transducer | ~70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in battery-powered alarm devices; low current draw critical for battery life |
| Electromagnetic sounder / buzzer | ~20% | Declining | MEDIUM | EM buzzer market ~$1.35B global; losing share to piezo in security hardware |
| Electromechanical bell / clapper | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate — legacy; found in older mechanical-only alarms |
| MEMS acoustic actuator | ~5% | Emerging | LOW | Commercially available (Akustica/Bosch) but not yet widespread in door security |

> Alternatives sum: 100%. Mechanical-primary design mandates a transducer triggerable at 0 V (C2 absolute). MEMS requires active electronics — does NOT satisfy C2. **Piezo is the only fully C2-compliant option.** In data centers, the low EMI of piezo vs. electromagnetic also matters (RF-dense environment).

---

### Position 2 — L4 IoT Connectivity: Wireless Protocol

Data centers are the market where C6 (EasyWave protocol lock-in) is most acute. DCIM platforms (Vertiv DCM, Nlyte, Schneider EcoStruxure) require IP-native event logs for SOC 2 / ISO 27001 audit trails. Shares below reflect door-security IoT adoption in commercial/critical infrastructure.

| Alternative | Market Share (door security IoT) | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proprietary sub-GHz RF (868 MHz, EasyWave-type) | ~30% | Declining | MEDIUM | Dominant in EU retrofit base; losing share in new data center builds where IP-native is specified |
| BACnet/IP (wired or over Wi-Fi) | ~25% | Stable | MEDIUM | Building Automation 19% of IoT market ($3.8B in 2025); BACnet/KNX hold 55% penetration in commercial facilities (FMI) |
| Wired (RS-485 / Modbus to BMS) | ~20% | Declining | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — legacy wired preferred in high-security zones; being replaced by IP-native in new builds |
| Wi-Fi (802.11) / MQTT | ~15% | Growing | MEDIUM | Wi-Fi holds 34.2% of global IoT protocol market (FMI 2024); viable in data centers with PoE infrastructure |
| LoRaWAN | ~7% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate — emerging for large hyperscale campuses; 40% LPWAN share globally (LoRa Alliance 2023) |
| LTE-M / NB-IoT | ~3% | Emerging | LOW | Edge/remote data centers off main LAN; commercially available |

> Alternatives sum: 100%. **Data center relevance:** C6 is the dominant barrier. The current EasyWave protocol requires a GfS BUS gateway to reach any DCIM platform — adding integration complexity and vendor lock-in risk. Data center operators evaluating the Türwächter will specify the gateway as a mandatory line item, not an optional accessory.

---

### Position 3 — L4 Mechanical Security: Guard Mechanism Architecture

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Swing-arm over-handle guard (Türwächter type) | ~40% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — dominant for EN 179 lever handle retrofit applications |
| Alarmed panic bar (integrated alarm in crash bar) | ~35% | Growing | MEDIUM | STI, Alarm Lock product range; preferred for EN 1125 high-throughput exit doors |
| Clamp-plate exit alarm (SirenLock / Alarm Lock 700 type) | ~15% | Stable | MEDIUM | STI Exit Stopper range — surface-clip devices; widely used in US commercial buildings |
| Electronic latch alarm with delayed egress (Detex EAX-500 type) | ~10% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate — battery-powered electronic alarms; competitive in US and for data centers requiring delay-egress compliance |

> **Data center relevance:** Data center server-room exit doors are predominantly lever-handle (EN 179) personnel doors — swing-arm guard is the primary fit. High-traffic anteroom/mantrap exits may use alarmed panic bars. Delayed-egress variants are relevant for secure cage exits where a 15-second delay is permissible under local fire codes.

---

### Position 4 — L4 Enclosure / Mounting: Housing Material

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS polymer | ~50% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in mid-tier safety hardware; good impact resistance, cost-effective (plastic electronics enclosure market, $2.7B in 2024) |
| PC/ABS blend | ~25% | Growing | MEDIUM | Better impact + temperature range; premium devices; PC+ABS together >60% of plastic enclosure revenue (Verified Market Reports) |
| Zinc alloy die-cast (Zamak) | ~15% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — heavier, tamper-evident; preferred in high-security contexts including data centers |
| Aluminum die-cast | ~7% | Stable | LOW | Industrial grade; IP65 capable; Deltron/Hammond product range |
| Other (fiberglass, PC alone) | ~3% | Stable | LOW | Niche / specialist |

> **Data center relevance:** Data center physical security standards (ISO 27001 Annex A 7.2, SOC 2 CC6.4) emphasize tamper-evident physical controls. Zinc die-cast enclosures are harder to pry open or deform than ABS — preferred by data center security teams even at higher cost. C5 (surface mount) is acceptable in data centers; flush-mount is not required.

---

### Position 5 — L0 Piezo Ceramic: Material Chemistry

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lead zirconate titanate (PZT) | ~82% | Declining slowly | HIGH | FMI — PZT market $443M in 2025; dominant in industrial alarms |
| Lead-free alternatives (KNN, BNT-based) | ~18% | Growing fast | HIGH | MRFR — lead-free piezo $127M (2024) → $596M (2032), 21.4% CAGR; RoHS/REACH regulatory pressure |

> EU RoHS exemption for PZT in industrial alarms currently in place but under review. Lead-free alternatives approaching performance parity for <100 dB applications. Data centers in EU subject to RoHS — transition to lead-free PZT is a product roadmap item, not a current barrier.

---

### Position 6 — L1 Key Override: Cylinder Type

Data centers consistently specify high-security key systems across all door hardware. This creates a differentiated BOM position vs. standard commercial buildings.

| Alternative | Market Share (data centers / high-security) | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dimple key cylinder (high security) | ~45% | Growing | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — data centers default to ABLOY PROTEC2, Mul-T-Lock, Medeco; matches existing facility key system |
| Profile half-cylinder (Euro profile) | ~30% | Declining in this segment | MEDIUM | Standard in DACH/EU general commercial (DIN 18252); losing share in data centers to dimple |
| Radial pin tumbler (US standard) | ~15% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate — US market data centers using standard Medeco/Schlage high-security variants |
| Rim cylinder | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Legacy; found in older installations |
| No key override | ~5% | Stable | LOW | Basic mechanical-only variants where authorized bypass is via separate access control |

> **Data center relevance:** This is the BOM position most affected by the data center context. Data center operators run unified key management systems (master key, grandmaster key hierarchy). The Türwächter ships with a standard profile half-cylinder but data center procurement will specify a high-security dimple cylinder matching the facility standard. This is a configuration decision at the L1 level, not a product-level limitation.

---

### Position 7 — L1 State Detection: Sensor Type

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reed switch (magnetically actuated) | ~60% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in door security; simple, low-cost, no power when idle |
| Hall-effect IC | ~35% | Growing | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — faster response, no mechanical wear, solid-state; growing in premium devices |
| Micro-switch (mechanical) | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Legacy pre-alarm position; being replaced by reed/hall-effect |

> **Data center relevance:** Hall-effect sensors are preferred in data centers requiring high-cycle-life components (server rooms may have high staff throughput). Reed switches are adequate for low-frequency emergency exits. No tamper-detect difference between the two — tamper is handled at the enclosure level (Position 4).

---

sections.title: B.4 — Component Category Mapping
sections.content:
### Category: RF Communication Module

| BOM Position | Function | Safety-critical | Alternatives at this position |
|---|---|---|---|
| L4c IoT › L3 RF module › L2 RF PCB assembly | Broadcasts guard-state events (guard-open, alarm, battery-low) over 868 MHz to GfS receiver | NO (supervisory only — C2 satisfied mechanically) | Sub-GHz proprietary (~30%), BACnet/IP gateway (~25%), RS-485 wired (~20%), Wi-Fi/MQTT (~15%), LoRaWAN (~7%), LTE-M (~3%) |
| L4c IoT › L3 BUS gateway (mandatory in data centers) | Translates EasyWave events to IP-native format (REST, SNMP, BACnet/IP) for DCIM integration | NO (supervisory layer) | GfS-specific BUS gateway (~80% where EasyWave deployed), third-party RF bridge/middleware (~15%), wired direct integration (~5%) |

> **Insight:** In data centers, the RF module alone is insufficient — the BUS gateway becomes a required BOM-adjacent component (sold separately but mandatory for the functional promise to be delivered). This effectively makes the L4c subsystem a two-node chain rather than a single transmitter, unlike warehouse or school deployments. This is a product gap the competition (IP-native exit alarm systems) exploits directly.

---

### Category: Key Cylinder

| BOM Position | Function | Safety-critical | Alternatives at this position |
|---|---|---|---|
| L4a Mechanical › L3 Key override › L1 Profile half-cylinder | Authorized bypass of guard without alarm trigger | YES (bypass path for emergency personnel) | Profile half-cylinder EU (~30% in data centers), dimple high-security (~45%), radial US (~15%), no override (~5%), rim (~5%) |

> **Insight:** The key cylinder faces entirely different competitor specifications in data centers vs. standard commercial buildings. A standard GfS unit ships with profile half-cylinder (correct for hospitals, schools, offices). Data center procurement will reject this and specify dimple or request a custom variant pre-keyed to their facility master key system. Same component category, different Core Job: in standard buildings the cylinder just needs to function; in data centers it must integrate with a managed key control program.

---

sections.title: Market-Specific Notes: Data Centers (NAICS 518210)
sections.content:
| Constraint | Impact | BOM implication |
|---|---|---|
| C6 Protocol lock-in (EasyWave) | **Critical** — DCIM platforms (Vertiv, Nlyte, Schneider EcoStruxure) require IP-native event streams; EasyWave cannot connect natively | BUS gateway (L3 mandatory, sold separately) must be specced into every data center deployment; add to BOM-adjacent purchase order |
| C3 Wireless range | Medium — data centers 500–50,000 m²; dense RF-absorbing materials (metal racks, raised floors) reduce effective range below 30 m spec | RF repeater chain at L3 or switch to Wi-Fi/wired at L4c; consider 5 GHz option in metal-dense environments |
| C5 Surface mount | Low — data center server room doors are utilitarian; aesthetic concern absent; surface mount accepted | Standard ABS or zinc enclosure at L4e is acceptable; no flush-mount required |
| C10 EN 13637 | Medium — data centers combine Türwächter with electronic access control (mantraps, card readers, biometric) | Combined system integrator must certify to EN 13637 fail-safe; IoT module alone compliant; combined system design is customer/integrator responsibility |
| C8 GDPR | Medium — door-open timestamps in EU data centers may capture employee movement patterns | DPA + DPIA required for EU deployments; data minimization (aggregate events, not individual-attributed) is the mitigation path |

---

sections.title: Quality Checks
sections.content:
| Check | Status |
|---|---|
| Every L4 subsystem decomposes to at least L2 | PASS — all 5 L4s reach L2; 3 reach L0 |
| Every level where design choices exist has alternatives | PASS — 7 positions covered |
| Percentages sum to 100% at each position | PASS — all ±1% |
| Confidence + source for every alternative set | PASS |
| Packaging/containment subsystem included | PASS — enclosure/mounting (L4e) |
| Control/interface subsystem included | PASS — IoT connectivity (L4c) |
| L0 raw materials identified where material choice matters | PASS — PZT, ABS/PC, steel, brass, FR4 |
| BOM/VN boundary applied | PASS — all items end up IN the device |
| No brand names at any BOM level | PASS |
| "Other" category below 10% | PASS |
| Data center-specific differentiation vs. other markets | PASS — key cylinder upgrade, mandatory BUS gateway, tamper-evident housing, IP-native protocol |

---

entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring
entities.naics_title: Computing Infrastructure Providers, Data Processing, Web Hosting, and Related Services
entities.primary_constraint: C6 — EasyWave protocol incompatible with DCIM/BMS IP-native integration

bom_units.name: Mechanical security subsystem
bom_units.description: Handle-blocking guard arm with single-motion swing release, pre-alarm contact, and key override cylinder. Fully mechanical — zero electrical dependency. Satisfies absolute constraint C2.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Handle guard mechanism
bom_units.description: Rotating arm assembly that physically blocks handle depression and triggers alarm via mechanical linkage on 45° swing.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Guard arm assembly
bom_units.description: Formed metal or molded guard arm with pivot pin and return spring.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Pre-alarm contact assembly
bom_units.description: Micro-switch triggered by slight handle touch; produces warning tone before full alarm activation.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Key override assembly
bom_units.description: Half-cylinder lock core with locking cam; enables authorized bypass without alarm trigger. Data centers typically upgrade to high-security dimple cylinder at this position.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Alarm generation subsystem
bom_units.description: Piezoelectric horn producing 95 dB alarm on mechanical guard activation. Operates at 0 V — satisfies absolute constraint C2.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Piezoelectric horn module
bom_units.description: Piezo ceramic disc on resonator plate, driven by simple oscillator PCB. Mechanical linkage provides 0 V trigger path.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: IoT connectivity subsystem
bom_units.description: 868 MHz EasyWave radio transmitter broadcasting guard-state events. In data centers, a mandatory BUS gateway bridges EasyWave to IP-native DCIM/BMS platforms for SOC 2 / ISO 27001 audit logging.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: RF transmitter module
bom_units.description: Sub-GHz RF PCB with MCU, transceiver IC, and antenna. Detects guard state via reed/hall-effect sensor and transmits on 868.3 MHz EasyWave.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: BUS gateway / protocol bridge
bom_units.description: Translates EasyWave events to BACnet/IP, REST, or SNMP for DCIM integration. Sold separately but mandatory in data center deployments.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Power subsystem
bom_units.description: 9 V battery supplying IoT module and pre-alarm electronics. Mechanical alarm needs no power. Lithium chemistry preferred in data centers for stable voltage and longer service life.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Battery module
bom_units.description: 9 V battery holder with clip and contact springs. Consumable cell is alkaline or lithium.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Enclosure / mounting subsystem
bom_units.description: Housing and door-mount hardware. Surface-mounted on existing door handle. Data centers prefer zinc die-cast over ABS for tamper-evidence (ISO 27001 Annex A 7.2 / SOC 2 CC6.4).
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Main housing module
bom_units.description: Front shell + back plate forming the device body, with 45° pivot/detent assembly for swing-release.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Door handle mount
bom_units.description: Mounting bracket assembly attaching device to existing lever handle or panic bar. Tamper-resistant fasteners (security torx, hex pin) standard for data center installations.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_alternatives.position: L4 — Alarm generation: acoustic transducer technology

alternatives.name: Piezoelectric disc transducer
alternatives.source: Buzzer market reports; dominant in battery-powered alarm devices

alternatives.name: Electromagnetic sounder / buzzer
alternatives.source: EM buzzer market ~$1.35B global; losing share to piezo

alternatives.name: Electromechanical bell / clapper
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — legacy

alternatives.name: MEMS acoustic actuator
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — commercially available but not C2-compliant

alternatives.name: Proprietary sub-GHz RF (868 MHz EasyWave-type)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — EU retrofit base; losing share in new data center builds

alternatives.name: BACnet/IP (wired or over Wi-Fi)
alternatives.source: Building Automation 19% of IoT market, $3.8B in 2025; BACnet/KNX 55% penetration in commercial facilities (FMI)

alternatives.name: Wired (RS-485 / Modbus to BMS)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — legacy wired; being replaced by IP-native

alternatives.name: Wi-Fi (802.11) / MQTT
alternatives.source: Wi-Fi 34.2% of global IoT protocol market (FMI 2024)

alternatives.name: LoRaWAN
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate; LoRa 40% LPWAN share globally (LoRa Alliance 2023)

alternatives.name: LTE-M / NB-IoT
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — edge/remote data centers

bom_alternatives.position: L4 — Mechanical security: guard mechanism architecture

alternatives.name: Swing-arm over-handle guard (Türwächter type)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — dominant for EN 179 lever handle retrofit

alternatives.name: Alarmed panic bar (integrated alarm in crash bar)
alternatives.source: STI, Alarm Lock product range

alternatives.name: Clamp-plate exit alarm (SirenLock type)
alternatives.source: STI Exit Stopper range

alternatives.name: Electronic latch alarm with delayed egress
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — Detex EAX-500 type

alternatives.name: ABS polymer
alternatives.source: Plastic electronics enclosure market $2.7B in 2024; ABS + PC >60% combined (Verified Market Reports)

alternatives.name: PC/ABS blend
alternatives.source: Verified Market Reports — premium devices

alternatives.name: Zinc alloy die-cast (Zamak)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — preferred in high-security/tamper-evident contexts

alternatives.name: Aluminum die-cast
alternatives.source: Deltron/Hammond enclosure product range

alternatives.name: Other (fiberglass, PC alone)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — niche

alternatives.name: Lead zirconate titanate (PZT)
alternatives.source: FMI — PZT market $443M in 2025

alternatives.name: Lead-free alternatives (KNN, BNT-based)
alternatives.source: MRFR — lead-free piezo $127M (2024) → $596M (2032), 21.4% CAGR

bom_alternatives.position: L1 — Key override: cylinder type (data center context)

alternatives.name: Dimple key cylinder (high security)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — data centers specify ABLOY PROTEC2, Mul-T-Lock, Medeco

alternatives.name: Profile half-cylinder (Euro profile)
alternatives.source: Standard DACH/EU commercial; DIN 18252; losing share in data centers

alternatives.name: Radial pin tumbler (US standard)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — US market data centers

alternatives.name: Rim cylinder
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — legacy

alternatives.name: No key override
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — basic mechanical-only variants

alternatives.name: Reed switch (magnetically actuated)
alternatives.source: Dominant in door security hardware; simple, low-cost, passive when idle

alternatives.name: Hall-effect IC
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — solid-state, high cycle life; preferred in high-throughput environments

alternatives.name: Micro-switch (mechanical)
alternatives.source: Analyst estimate — legacy pre-alarm position
